coredata.dc:title: Fluorescence copolymer-based dual-signal monitoring tyrosinase activity and its inhibitor screening via blue-green emission transformation
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Can
Pang, Lihua
Wang, Rui
Zou, Changpeng
Ruan, Guotong
Sun, Yujie
Zhang, Chengwu
Yu, Haidong
Li, Lin
Liu, Jinhua
coredata.dc:description: Tyrosinase (TYR) is a crucial enzyme in melanin metabolism and catecholamine production, its abnormal overexpression is closely associated with many human diseases involving melanoma cancer, vitiligo, Parkinson's disease and so on. Herein, a dual-signal fluorescence sensing system for monitoring TYR activity is constructed depending on the transformation of blue-green fluorescence emission of copolymer. The developed sensing system is based on TYR catalyzing the hydroxylation of mono-phenol to o-diphenol and the conversion of fluorescence copolymer (FCP) blue emission (430 nm) and green emission (535 nm) in the presence of PEI. In the system, both blue and green emission exhibit a high selectivity and sensitivity (S/B up to 300 and 30 for blue and green emission, respectively) toward TYR in the range from 0.5 to 2.5 U/mL with the detection limit of 0.002 U/mL and 0.06 U/mL, respectively. Additionally, this assay is used to detect TYR in human serum with excellent recovery even at 30% human serum concentrations. Furthermore, it still has been successfully applied to TYR inhibitor screening by taking kojic acid as a model. We believe that our developed sensor has great potential application in TYR-associated disease diagnosis and treatment and drug discovery.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Copolymer
Fluorescence transformation
Dual-signal
Tyrosinase
Levodopa

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of TYR activity by in situ synthesis of FCP
Determination of TYR in human serum samples
In situ fluorescent sensing system for TYR inhibitor screening

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Schematic representation of TYR-triggered in situ synthesis of FCP. (B) The fluorescence spectra of mixture of L-tyrosine and PEI for 10 min and 120 min (1, 2), mixture of TYR and PEI for 10 min and 120 min (3, 4), mixture of L-tyrosine and TYR for 10 min and 120 min (5, 6), mixture of TYR-incubated L-tyrosine and PEI for 10 min and 120 min (7, 8). (C) TEM image of green FCP. Insets: The corresponding particle size distribution histograms.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of blue FCP at different concentrations of TYR at 345 nm excitation. (B) Fluorescence spectra of green FCP at different concentrations of TYR at 385 nm excitation. (C) Linear curve of blue emission. (D) Linear curve of green emission.

(A) Selectivity investigation of the sensing system for TYR. 0, Blank; 1, TYR; 2, Decarboxylase; 3, Trypsin; 4, Pepsin; 5, BSA; 6, GSH; 7, Cytc, respectively. (B) Corresponding photographs under 365 nm UV light.
The binding mode of (A) tyrosine and (B) kojic acid with the active site of tyrosinase.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of blue FCP with increasing concentrations of kojic acid. (B) Relationship between the fluorescence intensity ratio (1-F1/F0) of blue FCP and kojic acid concentrations. (C) Fluorescence spectra of green FCP with increasing concentrations of kojic acid. (D) Relationship between the fluorescence intensity ratio (1-F1/F0) green FCP and kojic acid concentrations.
Schematic illustration of detection strategy for TYR based on the blue and green FCP.
Analytical results of TYR activity in human serum samples.

head.ce:title: Fluorescence copolymer-based dual-signal monitoring tyrosinase activity and its inhibitor screening via blue-green emission transformation

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Flexible Electronics (KLOFE) and Institute of Advanced Materials (IAM), Jiangsu National Synergetic Innovation Center for Advanced Materials (SICAM), Nanjing Tech University (NanjingTech), 30 South Puzhu Road, Nanjing 211816, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Flexible Electronics (KLOFE) and Institute of Advanced Materials (IAM)
Jiangsu National Synergetic Innovation Center for Advanced Materials (SICAM)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Flexible Electronics (KLOFE) and Institute of Advanced Materials (IAM), Jiangsu National Synergetic Innovation Center for Advanced Materials (SICAM), Nanjing Tech University (NanjingTech), 30 South Puzhu Road, Nanjing 211816, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Tyrosinase (TYR) is a crucial enzyme in melanin metabolism and catecholamine production, its abnormal overexpression is closely associated with many human diseases involving melanoma cancer, vitiligo, Parkinson's disease and so on. Herein, a dual-signal fluorescence sensing system for monitoring TYR activity is constructed depending on the transformation of blue-green fluorescence emission of copolymer. The developed sensing system is based on TYR catalyzing the hydroxylation of mono-phenol to o-diphenol and the conversion of fluorescence copolymer (FCP) blue emission (430 nm) and green emission (535 nm) in the presence of PEI. In the system, both blue and green emission exhibit a high selectivity and sensitivity (S/B up to 300 and 30 for blue and green emission, respectively) toward TYR in the range from 0.5 to 2.5 U/mL with the detection limit of 0.002 U/mL and 0.06 U/mL, respectively. Additionally, this assay is used to detect TYR in human serum with excellent recovery even at 30% human serum concentrations. Furthermore, it still has been successfully applied to TYR inhibitor screening by taking kojic acid as a model. We believe that our developed sensor has great potential application in TYR-associated disease diagnosis and treatment and drug discovery.

ce:section.ce:para: Tyrosinase (TYR) is widely distributed in animal and plants tissues and plays a significant role in a great deal of physiological processes, e.g. melanogenesis in mammals, human aging, insect cuticle sclerotization, plants browning reactions, and so on [1–4]. As a unique copper-containing enzyme with dual catalytic function, it can efficiently catalyze the hydroxylation of mono-phenol to o-diphenol as well as the oxidation of o-diphenol to o-quinones in the existence of molecular oxygen [5–7]. Thus, TYR is the key to melanin synthesis in organisms, in which it specifically catalyzes the oxidation of tyrosine to dopamine and further to dopaquinone, and then polymerized to melanin [8–10]. In the clinical research, TYR has been considered not only as a dependable biomarker for melanoma cancer due to its overexpression, but also as a credible biomarker for vitiligo owing to as a self-antigen [11–14]. In addition, TYR is also an influential factor Parkinson's disease due to its major implication on dopamine neurotoxicity and neurodegeneration [15–17]. In view of the important role of TYR in pathophysiology, it is of great significance for clinical diagnosis and treatment of tyrosine related diseases to monitor its activity.

Up to present, all kinds of sensing strategy have been developed for the determination of TYR activity, including electrochemical assay [18–21], colorimetric assay [22,23] and fluorescence assay [24,25]. Compared with electrochemical assay and colorimetric assay, fluorescence assay has drawn much attention for monitoring TYR activity due to its simple, fast, high sensitivity and stability as well as noninvasive imaging [26–30]. Qian group developed a simple and real-time method for the detection of TYR activity and screening of its inhibitors on the basis of the biocompatible dopamine functionalized carbon quantum dots [31]. Yang group reported a novel fluorescent sensor based on polymethyldopa nanoparticles to detect TYR activity utilizing a commercially available methyltyrosine as the substrate [32]. Sun group designed a two-photon-excitation and dual-emission semiconducting polymer dot as a ratiometric sensor for monitoring and bioimaging of TYR activity [33]. Although such fluorescence methods for TYR activity detection has been exploited, some disadvantages exist, including single signal, complex synthesis process, and complicated separation and purification processes of fluorescent probes, and limit their further applications [34–38]. Therefore, it is an urgent need to build a multi-signal and convenient fluorescence detection method for monitoring TYR activity.

In this work, we developed a dual-signal sensing system for monitoring TYR activity by using in situ formation of fluorescence copolymer (FCP). This sensing system was based on the principle that TYR can catalyze the oxidation of L-tyrosine to levodopa and further react with PEI to form FCP, accompanying with a conversion of blue emission (430 nm) to green emission (535 nm). It exhibited a high sensitivity and selectivity toward TYR both in blue and green emission channel. Additionally, our sensing system was applied to detect TYR at serum with good recovery even at high serum concentrations up to 30%. Furthermore, the dual-signal sensing system could also be used for screening TYR inhibitor by taking kojic acid as a model. Consequently, the proposed dual-signal sensing system provided a promising method for TYR activity monitoring and its inhibitor screening.

Levodopa, L-tyrosine, Decarboxylase, Trypsin, Pepsin, Bovine Serum Albumin (BSA), Glutathione (GSH), Cytochrome C (Cytc), and Kojic acid were purchased from Aladdin reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Polyethyleneimine (PEI) was purchased from Wanqing Chemical Glass Instruments Co., Ltd. (Nanjing, China). Tyrosinase (TYR) was purchased from Heiyi Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Nanjing, China). Human blood sample was purchased from Youqing Biological Reagent Co., Ltd. (Nanjing, China). All chemicals were analytical reagent grade and used without further purification.

An F-7100 fluorescence spectrophotometer (HITACHI, Japan) was used to record the fluorescence spectra with a xenon lamp as the excitation source and a standard 10 × 10 mm quartz cell. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were acquired utilizing a JEM-2010 UHR transmission electron microscopy (JEOL, Japan). Ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ.cm) was obtained using a Milli-Q Advantage A10 purification system (Millipore S.A, Bedford, USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of TYR activity by in situ synthesis of FCP
ce:section.ce:para: For detection of TYR activity, 500 μM L-tyrosine (5 μL 100 mM) and TYR with different activities varying from 0 to 5 U/mL were added into 50 μL of Tris-HCl buffer (10 mM, pH 7.4). After incubation for 30 min at 37 °C, 500 μL 50 mg/mL of PEI was added. The above mixture solutions were maintained at room temperature. Then the blue fluorescence spectra were recorded with the excitation of 345 nm after 10 min of mixing, and the green fluorescence spectra were recorded with the excitation of 385 nm after 120 min of mixing.

The selectivity of TYR was obtained in comparison with Decarboxylase, Trypsin, Pepsin, BSA, GSH, Cytc under the same condition. The concentrations of TYR and enzymes/proteins/Cytc were 4 U/mL or 100 μg/mL, respectively.
Diluted human serum (1%, 5%, 10%, 20%, and 30%) was used in this system for the detection of TYR activity in real samples.

For inhibitor screening, 5 U/mL of TYR and kojic acid solutions with different concentrations varying from 0 to 200 μM were added into 50 μL of Tris-HCl buffer (10 mM, pH 7.4). After kept for 30 min at 37 °C, 5 μL 100 mM L-tyrosine was added. After incubation for 30 min at 37 °C again, 500 μL 50 mg/mL of PEI was added. We kept the above mixed solutions at room temperature. Then the blue fluorescence spectra were recorded with the excitation of 345 nm after 10 min of mixing, and the green fluorescence spectra were recorded with the excitation of 385 nm after 120 min of mixing.

In our previous work, a novel fluorescence sensing system in the basis of in situ formation of blue and green emission-transformed fluorescent copolymer (FCP) for the detection of levodopa was successfully constructed [39]. Gradually increased blue and green fluorescence of FCP could be observed with the increase of levodopa concentrations (Fig. S1). As a continuation of our research work, it was proved that the constructed sensing system could be further used for the monitoring of TYR activity (Scheme 1 ). Generally, in the existence of oxygen and TYR, L-tyrosine could be oxidized to levodopa specifically, and further reacted with PEI to produce blue and green FCP (Fig. 1A) [39–43]. As shown in Fig. 1B, the mixture of L-tyrosine and PEI, and the mixture of TYR and PEI had no obvious fluorescence both in 10 min and 120 min, which suggested that PEI could not react with L-tyrosine or TYR. Also, TYR-incubated L-tyrosine solution did not generate fluorescence both in 10 min and 120 min. However, in the presence of PEI, TYR-incubated L-tyrosine solution formed a strong fluorescence peak at 430 nm after reaction 10 min (blue curve), and strong fluorescence peak at 535 nm after reaction 120 min (green curve), implying that FCP could be formed by mixing the PEI and TYR-incubated L-tyrosine. The results indicated that L-tyrosine could be hydroxylated by TYR-catalyzed specifically. Overall, in the presence of TYR and O2, L-tyrosine could be oxidized to levodopa and react with PEI to produce FCP (about ~4 nm, Fig. 1C) of blue-green emission transformation according to different reaction times. Thus, the method of TYR-triggered in situ formation of FCP via blue-green emission transformation was proposed, and it could be applied for dual-signal monitoring TYR activity.

In addition, a possible mechanism for the conversion of FCP from blue emission (430 nm) to green emission (535 nm) in the presence of PEI was provided. At room temperature, levodopa can be oxidized to its quinone derivative slowly and then further oxidized to dopachrome, which could self-polymerize to non-fluorescent polylevodopa (Fig. S2A) [39]. When PEI provided an amine-rich environment, the oxidation of levodopa was accelerated, thus levodopa and PEI reacted quickly to produce blue FCP. And with the extension of the incubation time, the levodopa in the blue FCP could be further oxidized and cyclized, resulting in an increase in the conjugation system (Fig. S2B). The increase of conjugation system could induce a red shift in the emission wavelength [44], and thus the shift from blue to green emission could be observed (Fig. S3). We have also found that with increasing oxidation time, the FCP solution changed from colorless to yellow and a new absorption peak appeared around 420 nm (Fig. S4), indicative of the accumulation and further polymerization and oxidation of the quinone [45]. Therefore, the conversion of FCP from blue to green emission may be related to the increase of conjugation system.

To obtain the optimum condition for detection of TYR activity, we investigated the effect of PEI concentration, pH and L-tyrosine concentration, and the incubation time of L-tyrosine with TYR. Both the fluorescence intensity of FCP at 430 nm and 535 nm increased as the PEI concentration increased (Fig. S5A, B). As shown in Fig. S6A, B, the pH value was selected as 7.4, and the incubation time of L-tyrosine with TYR was optimized to 0.5 h. As the L-tyrosine concentration increased, both the fluorescence intensity of FCP at 430 nm and 535 nm increased (Fig. S7A, B). Finally, 500 μM L-tyrosine was set as the concentration of substrate.

In our sensing system, TYR-incubated L-tyrosine solution could exhibit blue and green emission under the effect of PEI, and the fluorescence intensity has been proved to be related to the TYR concentration. Under the optimal conditions, TYR activity monitoring was carried out. Based on in situ formation of FCP, gradually increased fluorescence intensities at 430 nm and 535 nm were recorded with the concentrations of TYR varying from 0 to 5 U/mL, and two linear curves were obtained (Figs. 2A, B, S8A, B). The Fig. 2C, D illustrated the good linear relationship between fluorescence intensities ratio (F1/F0, where F1 and F0 were the fluorescence intensities in the presence and absence of TYR, respectively) and the concentration of TYR in the range of 0.5 to 2.5 U/mL with the limit of detection (LOD) of 0.002 U/mL in blue emission (F1/F0 = −49.81 + 135.82 CTYR, R2 = 0.9703), and the LOD of 0.06 U/mL in green emission (F1/F0 = −4.24 + 13.49 CTYR, R2 = 0.9785). The linear range and LOD of our proposed method were compared with those reported previously in Table S1 [46–51]. In addition, it can be observed that the blue and green fluorescence gradually increased as the TYR activity increased under 365 nm UV light (insets of Fig. 2A, B).

In order to study the selectivity of our proposed sensing system, some interfering substances containing decarboxylase, trypsin, pepsin, BSA, GSH and Cytc were studied under the same conditions, and then the fluorescence spectra were recorded. As shown in Fig. 3A, no apparent fluorescence response was observed for these interfering substances, only TYR showed strong blue and green fluorescence peak at 430 nm for reaction 10 min (enhancement about 300-fold) and 535 nm for reaction 120 min (enhancement about 30-fold), respectively. And the enhancement values of F1/F0 for the others were about 1 to the blank, implying the good specificity of our method in blue and green channel toward TYR. Furthermore, the conversion of blue and green fluorescence emission can be observed under 365 nm UV light only in the presence of TYR (Fig. 3B).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of TYR in human serum samples
ce:section.ce:para: To further study the potential application of our method in complex samples, the developed dual-signal TYR sensing system was investigated in diluted human serum samples. Different concentration (1%, 5%, 10%, 20%, and 30%) human serum samples were investigated for detection of TYR activity in blue and green signal channel and the results of TYR activity were summarized in Table 1 . The results demonstrated that the blue emission for TYR activity determination had a good recovery ranging from 85% to 101% in low concentration human serum samples (1%, and 5%), owing to the strong fluorescence and fast response in blue channel. While because of less background interference in green channel, green emission exhibited a satisfactory recovery ranging from 98% to 106.7% in high concentration human serum samples (10%, 20%, and 30%). Therefore, comprehensive evaluation of dual-signal can select the most suitable method according to specific needs for TYR activity monitoring. And the blue and green emission displayed rapid response time and good recovery even at high human serum concentrations of up to 30%, indicating that our proposed dual-signal sensing system can be applied in real samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In situ fluorescent sensing system for TYR inhibitor screening
ce:section.ce:para: As a copper-containing polyphenol oxidase, the catalytic activity of TYR could be effectively reduced by its inhibitors such as some copper chelators [52–55]. TYR inhibitors had been used for the treatment of common pigmented skin diseases. Given the importance of TYR inhibitor, our fluorescent sensor was further used for the screening of TYR inhibitor. Kojic acid, a well-known TYR inhibitor, was selected as a typical representative to check out the screening function of our sensing system. The molecular docking simulations were carried out in Fig. 4 [56–58]. The binding free energy of TYR to tyrosine and kojic acid were −6.02 and −6.38 kcal/mol, respectively, which indicated that kojic acid possessed a stronger binding capacity to TYR than tyrosine. In the current detection of TYR process, the presence of kojic acid could block the oxidation of L-tyrosine to levodopa and couldn't produce the FCP, and then the inhibition effect could be obtained. As depicted in Fig. 5 , the fluorescence intensity of FCP gradually decreased as the concentration of kojic acid increasing from 0 μM to 100 μM, and no obvious fluorescence change could be observed when the concentration of kojic acid was over 100 μM, implying that TYR activity had been effectively inhibited by kojic acid. The IC50 values of kojic acid were calculated to be 11.6 μM for blue channel and 13.6 μM for green channel, which had the same order of magnitude as the previously reported results of TYR inhibitor [29]. Thus, the further development of in situ dual-signal system showed excellent prospects for TYR inhibitor screening.

In this study, we constructed a blue-green fluorescence emission-transformed copolymer sensing system for dual-signal detection of TYR activity in the basis of in situ formation of FCP. Our sensing system possessed the following excellent characteristics. First, dual-signal detection of the sensing system was achieved by the fluorescence emission transition from blue to green in FCP. Second, the convenient method toward TYR activity monitoring exhibited a high selectivity and sensitivity ranging from 0.5 to 2.5 U/mL with the LOD of 0.002 U/mL and 0.06 U/mL in blue and green emission channel, respectively. Third, it can be directly used to determinate TYR activity in human serum samples (even at human serum concentrations of up to 30%) with a favorable recovery. Finally, the sensing system has been further extended to detect TYR inhibitor and the IC50 values of kojic acid were calculated to be 11.6 and 13.6 μM for blue and green emission, respectively. Therefore, our developed dual-signal sensing system has realized the determination of TYR activity and its inhibitors, which can be expected to play a good potential in the monitoring, diagnosis and treatment of TYR-associated disease.
CC: Started the work, synthesis, properties studies and initial manuscript writing.
LP: Properties studies and test.
RW: Properties studies and test.
CZ: Properties studies and test.
GR: Properties studies and test.
YS: Properties studies and test.
CZ: Revised the manuscript and gave some suggestions.
HY: Revised the manuscript and revised the manuscript.
LL: Revised the manuscript and revised the manuscript.
JL: Overall supervise and writing the final manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: There are no conflicts of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the financial support from the National Science Foundation of China (Grant nos. 21675085, 21505072, 81672508, 61505076), Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province for Distinguished Young Scholars (BK20170041, BK20170042) and the Opening Project of State Key Laboratory of Chemo/Biosensing and Chemometrics of Hunan University (Grant nos. 2018008), Key University Science Research Project of Jiangsu Province (No. 16KJA180004).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2020.119028.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: 3-Aminophenyl boronic acid-functionalized quantum dot-based ratiometric fluorescence sensor for the highly sensitive detection of tyrosinase activity

Zhang, 3-Aminophenyl boronic acid-functionalized quantum dot-based ratiometric fluorescence sensor for the highly sensitive detection of tyrosinase activity, ACS Sens. 2020, DOI: 10.1021/acssensors.0c00122.
